s2.q: The anatomical definition of contracted pelvis is:
options: A pelvis that interferes with normal childbirth
A pelvis in which one or more diameters is reduced below normal by at least 1 cm
A pelvis with a diagonal conjugate <10 cm
A pelvis with an outlet diameter <8 cm
s2.explain: Anatomical: diameters reduced ≥1 cm below normal. Obstetric definition adds: interferes with normal childbirth.

s2.q: Which definition of contracted pelvis is more clinically relevant?
options: The obstetric definition (interferes with the normal mechanism of labor)
s2.explain: The obstetric definition is more relevant because it couples the small diameter to interference with childbirth — this changes clinical management.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s2.q: Which of the following best defines this condition?
options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s2.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

options: Transversely contracted due to absence of 2 sacral alae
Asymmetric/obliquely contracted due to absence of one sacral ala
Sacrum made of 6 fused segments (high assimilation)
Sacrum made of 4 fused segments (low assimilation)
s3.explain: Naegele's = asymmetric (one sacral ala missing → oblique contraction). Robert's = bilateral (double Naegele) → transverse contraction.

s3.q: Which acquired condition causes contracted pelvis through metabolic bone disease?
s3.explain: Rickets/osteomalacia are metabolic diseases affecting pelvic bones. The others (kyphosis, scoliosis, spondylolisthesis) are vertebral column causes.

s3.q: Acquired causes of contracted pelvis are classified into three compartments: pelvic bones, vertebral column, and:
s3.explain: The B-V-L mnemonic: Bones (pelvic), Vertebrae (column), Legs (lower limb) = the 3 load-bearing columns shaping the pelvis.

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s3.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: Contracted pelvis with only the outlet affected is:
s4.explain: Outlet contraction is the rarest (~1%). Midpelvic contraction is more common. There are 4 levels: inlet, midpelvic, outlet, and both inlet+outlet.

s4.q: Pelvic inlet contraction is diagnosed when the diagonal conjugate is:
s4.explain: Inlet contraction: A-P diameter <10 cm OR transverse <12 cm. Diagnosed by diagonal conjugate <11.5 cm.

s4.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s4.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s4.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: In 1st degree contracted pelvis, the conjugate vera is:
s5.explain: Four degrees: 1st (10-11 cm, normal = 11 cm), 2nd (9-10 cm), 3rd (8-9 cm), 4th (<8 cm).

s5.q: A conjugate vera of 7.5 cm corresponds to which degree of pelvic contraction?
s5.explain: 4th degree: conjugate vera <8 cm. 1st: 10-11 cm, 2nd: 9-10 cm, 3rd: 8-9 cm, 4th: <8 cm.

options: Suspected CPD (cephalo-pelvic disproportion) or cases with previous CS for CPD
s6.explain: Radiological pelvimetry is indicated for suspected CPD and previous CS for CPD.

s6.q: Clinical pelvimetry includes assessment of all EXCEPT:
s6.explain: Clinical pelvimetry assesses diagonal conjugate, transverse diameter, bi-spinous, bi-tuberous, and outlet diameters. Fetal blood sampling is not pelvic assessment.

s6.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications

s6.q: Which clinical finding is most suggestive of this diagnosis?
options: Varies by condition — specific findings guide diagnosis
s6.explain: Clinical presentation differs by condition and guides diagnostic testing.

s7.q: A common complication of contracted pelvis is:
s7.explain: Complications include prolonged/obstructed labor, postpartum hemorrhage, rupture uterus, puerperal sepsis, vesicovaginal fistula, and fetal complications.

s7.q: Which of the following is a fetal complication of contracted pelvis?
s7.explain: Fetal: intracranial hemorrhage, nerve palsies, fractures, asphyxia, and neonatal death. Uterine rupture and VVF are maternal complications.

s7.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s7.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
Fetal well-being is closely linked to maternal condition — multiple fetal complications are possible.

s7.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s7.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s8.q: Management of contracted pelvis depends primarily on:
options: Degree of contraction and presence of CPD
s8.explain: Management depends on degree of contraction and presence of CPD. Trial of labor may be attempted for mild/non-CPD cases; severe cases require CS.

s8.q: In mild contracted pelvis without CPD, the recommended approach is:
options: Trial of labor under close monitoring with partograph
s8.explain: A trial of labor with close partograph monitoring is appropriate for mild cases without CPD. Severe contraction or definite CPD = cesarean section.

options: Depends on the specific condition and severity
s8.explain: Management approach varies — some conditions require surgery, others medical or conservative care.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s8.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
